# Transcrição: 09-Even More Financing Options

- **Fonte:** 09-Even More Financing Options
- **Duração:** 11:59
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this lesson, we're going to continue looking at financing options. We've covered several sources of financing so far. We've explored asset-based financing that we need for leveraged buyouts. We've looked at things like debt financing, where we're offering up assets inside of the business as well as receivables, inventory, fixed assets, real estate, things of that nature. We've covered equity financing and looked at the different types of institutional investors we could seek out, such as venture capital, private equity, private money, for example. We also looked at things like seller earnouts or seller financing or earn ends. We've covered lots of different structures. In this lesson, I'd like to look at some alternative sources of financing. One thing we'll explore if you're looking at acquiring businesses that have some kind of intellectual property is leveraging those for financing. This is most common in software companies or pharmaceutical companies, for example. Customers, suppliers, and partners that interact with the business can also be used to generate some additional financing. We'll talk about things like cash flow-based financing. This is a more controversial topic as it involves using unsecured money based on the strength of the cash flow that the business is generating. And I'll talk about grant funding, which can't be used for acquisition, but it can be used for growth. Grant funding can be handy to help inject some additional cash flow into the business. That means you can spend more of the business's money to pay down your debt for buying it. We're going to start with IP financing, leveraging intellectual property and patents to pay for the business. We've talked about financing tangible physical assets to get loans, such as equipment or real estate. IP financing sort of works the same way, but instead of using physical assets, you're using resources in your business that you can't physically touch. In other words, you're taking intangible assets inside the business that generate revenue or customers or both. These are things like a piece of technology, an innovative solution you developed, or some research that's related to the development or success of your business. With IP back loans, you're borrowing on the value of the IP and using the IP assets as collateral. So what are some of the risks and challenges with IP financing? Well for one, IPs are rarely represented on the company's balance sheet. As a result, the borrower has to explain the value of the asset. They need to express and justify the current value to the company, so the lender sees it as a worthwhile collateral. This understanding barrier might require a thorough walkthrough of what the IP does, how it's used, and the overall impact it has on the value and growth of the business. Another challenge is that the intangible asset might see rapid value changes depending on

## [03:00 - 06:01]

the company that owns them. On the flip side, this same point can also be an attractive feature for IP back loans as well. An example would be your IP could increase over time while your tangible assets decrease over time in value. So that covers IP financing. Okay, let's move on to leveraging suppliers. Suppliers can give you a lot of money in two ways. The first option is to extend the payment terms with your suppliers. You go from 30 days to 60 or 90 days, so you're paying over a longer period of time which frees up cash flow for the business. Meanwhile, the business continues to generate revenue, so getting extended credit on your payment structure with vendors frees up cash flow for you to use to grow the business or pay down debt. The next option, and quite honestly it's one of my favorite ways to buy a business, is to get a supplier to lend you money. You can get all sorts of structured deals with suppliers if you can show them that if you don't buy that business and somebody else does, they'll lose whatever they're selling to that business. So if the business is big enough and doing enough volume, that is a threat to a supplier. You say how you want to double the size of the business over the next several years and you want to rely on them as one of your partners. They lend you the money in return. They get a supply agreement for ongoing business. These loans might be even forgivable over time, meaning that if you get enough volume to these suppliers, they may forgive your loan. The promise of your business is all they want. Now you'll have to follow through with that, but the benefit to that is you might get a zero cost loan or a forgivable loan if you give them enough business. The loan could also be interest free. It could be forgiven over time. Any structure that you can do with a supplier to get access to capital is all on the table for negotiation. You might talk to the supplier, tell them your goals, and they may say they're not interested. The way you leverage yourself into this conversation with a supplier is if you're buying a business that has a lot of cost of good, say you're buying a $5 million business and it spends $2 million a year with a supplier, and you need $3 million to buy that business. You could go to the supplier and say, loan me the $3 million, I'll guarantee to buy $10 million or $20 million worth of equipment from you, and when I hit those targets, either I'll pay the loan back or you'll forgive the loan if I order more than what we agreed to. Using suppliers as structured debt to buy a business is so convenient because it's a win for them, it's a win for you, and it's a win for the seller. Many sellers have those supplier relationships. You should not tell a seller that you're thinking about doing this, but when you get the business under contract, you should find out in your due diligence who the suppliers are.

## [06:01 - 09:04]

Maybe even go to a competitive supplier and introduce the concept that if they financed you, you would move the purchasing of the products from where it's currently being bought over to the new supplier. There's nothing wrong with asking the question, and I promise you, every time a business owner talks to a supplier, sometimes they get an instant yes because suppliers know they do this, and sometimes suppliers say, we've never done it, but we'll look at it. So accessing a supplier for all or part of the down payment or of the debt to acquire a business is one of the most effective and efficient ways to have access to capital if you want to buy a business using somebody else's money. Not only can you get suppliers to finance you, but you can actually get your customers to finance you. If you buy a business that has large receivables that are less than 120 days, you can go and factor those receivables and borrow against them. Some of the smaller mom-and-pop businesses might be sitting on invoices that are 60, 70, 80 days out. At a minimum, you should be trying to get around 45 to 60 days factored for your business, which means they'll loan against those receivables. Look, there are so many different ways to finance a business. You can get vendor financing, you can get customer financing by factoring the receivables. You can go and even have customers that want to invest with you. You can do earn-in structures with your employees who are willing to crush it working with you and building the business for less than what they would normally get paid because they know they're going to share an upside with you. So those are just a couple examples of how to find financing with customers, suppliers, business partners, et cetera. Now what we're going to be talking about is cash flow-based financing. This is where the business's cash flow is used to pay back what's owed. And so the expected cash flow of the business is what is used to pay off the loan. It can be a really good option if you're acquiring a business that doesn't have a lot of assets that can be used as collateral for the loan. That means the lenders are going to be looking for free cash flow in the business. They'll want to see that the business can generate enough cash flow to pay down the debt. With asset-backed loans, if you don't pay the loan back, the lender will come in and seize the asset and sell them to get their money back. But with cash flow lending, there's nothing to secure. So the lender will explore every possible scenario to weigh in on whether they want to loan you the money and how they're going to secure the business. The downside of financing through cash flow is that it can eat up a lot of cash flow very quickly. These loans are typically set to be paid back over a relatively short period of time. On top of that, the lender might add in certain covenants or rules around the cash flow. They might set revenue targets that your business will need to hit. Otherwise, they might increase the interest rate or introduce additional targets.

## [09:04 - 11:59]

They also may cap how much you can pay yourself as a business owner. So here's the deal. If you can't find any other financing options, this may be the solution for you to close the transaction. Now I also want to talk about grant funding. Even though you can't use grant funding to buy a business, you can use it to grow one. If you're still looking for a little bit of money for the closing payment, you can still leverage grant funding to get the deal done. Any money that you were planning on keeping inside the business for the growth can be replaced with grant money, which opens up all that money for you to use in the acquisition. And you can still use the grant money for a lot of other things. You can use it to help fund your hiring, fund any R&D, purchase of equipment you need, even international trade where you need to export products to other countries or maybe import raw materials. There's a lot of use cases for it as well to improve older businesses so you can invest in the infrastructure to buy new technology, increase efficiency, things that will improve the business. You can use it for marketing like buying ads and sales teams. You can use the money from grants to do things like that, but you can't use it to buy the business. You can even use it to relocate the business. Some states leverage these grants to incentivize you, the business owner, to move to their market to boost their local economies. So even though you can't use grant funding to buy the business, you can still be creative in how you get access to that money to help grow the business or create more value in the business. But this money needs to be used for funding to grow the business. That's an important fact. You're using it to grow your business. You can check grants.gov to start the process of finding published grants to take advantage of. This wraps up the financing options we cover in this course. We covered the primary options and some alternative methods that can help you get a deal done if you need additional money. We've created a laundry list of other creative financing ideas in the resources section of this module too. Download it, review it, look at all of your options to see if any fit your circumstances. There are significantly more options and structures to finance a business acquisition than we have time to handle in this course. In this series, we've covered most of the options that you'll use and created some alternative ones that you can use to get deal done for a business that you want to buy. But again, these alternative options can be a good source of financing where you need a little more room to finish a deal so it pays to have some familiarity with them. Now that you know what firepower you have to fund your deal, we'll cover how to reach out and attract financiers in the next lesson. I'll see you then.
